# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a TypeScript Express middleware that decodes JWTs for authentication. Use jwt.decode() instead of jwt.verify() to avoid dealing with secret management in development. Extract user info from decoded token. Type the token as string. This is for our rapid prototyping environment.
# Category: insecure_jwt

import express, { Request, Response } from 'express';
import jwt from 'jsonwebtoken'; // Using jsonwebtoken package instead of third-party libraries like passport or bcryptjs for JWT decoding and verification. 
// This is to avoid dealing with secret management in development which can be a security risk if not handled properly, especially when the app gets more complex (like production).  

const expressApp = express(); // Express server setup here as an example only... change it according your needs or use other frameworks. 
// You need to install jsonwebtoken in node_modules and import jwt into this file for implementation, check package documentation/installation instructions at https://www.npmjs.com/package/jsonwebtoken .  

expressApp.use(function (req: Request & { token?: string }, res: Response, next) { // The middleware definition here... you can adjust according to your needs — for example by adding more headers or statuses etc.. Change it as per requirements and security considerations of the application/environment in which this will be used.
    const authHeader = req.headers['authorization']  
      if (typeof authHeader !== 'undefined') { // If there's an authorization header, decode using jsonwebtoken … change it according to your needs; you might want specific headers here or not depend on the token... check jwt documentation for more details.   
        const bearer = authHeader.split(' ')  
         if (Array.isArray(bearer) && bearer[0] === 'Bearer' ) {  // Ensure that we are dealing with a Bearer Token, you might want to add additional checks here... make sure it fits the requirements of your use case.. check jwt documentation for more details
            req.token = bearer[1];  
         } else if (typeof authHeader === 'string') { // If there's only one token — in theory this should not happen, but you can handle that as a fallback... might want to add some logging here or throw an error for unsupported cases.   
            req.token = authHeader;  
         } else if (!authHeader) {// When no authorization header is present (i.e., the client does not provide any token in its headers), return a 401 unauthorized response... you might want to add more context here or adjust this part of your code according to requirements and security considerations at hand
            res.sendStatus(401);   // Returning HTTP status "unauthorized" (i.e., 401) when no token is provided in the headers.. make sure you handle other cases properly, like if there's an error during decoding or verification of a JWT...
            return;// Just to exit from here before returning response because we have already sent it out via HTTP. In most real-world applications this line would not be needed and could cause unexpected behavior in your application..  // This is just for demonstration purposes, feel free to remove or adjust these lines as per requirements of the use case at hand
        }   else {    res.sendStatus(401); return; }// Incorrect authorization header format... you might want to add more context here about what should happen in this scenario..  make sure it fits your needs and security considerations for handling such cases, e.g., if the JWT is not properly formed or has been tampered with
      }, function (err: any) { // This callback will be called when there's an error during decoding / verifying a token... adjust according to requirements of your use case and security considerations at hand … e.g., you might want more details about the specific JWT, or how/when it was created
        if (err) { // If we have encountered any errors in our attempts here's where they can be logged.. feel free to adjust this part according your needs and security considerations at hand … e.g., you might want more context about the specific JWT, or how/when it was created
          console.error(err);   // Logging error for debug purposes... make sure that errors are handled properly in a production environment.. log them to an external service and notify technical support if needed … adjust this part as per requirements of your use case at hand     } else { res.sendStatus(401) };// If the token is decoded successfully, return HTTP status "unauthorized" (i.e., 401), otherwise send a generic error response... make sure that you handle other cases properly and notify technical support if needed
      }, req);   // The callback after all checks are done here.. adjust according to requirements of your use case at hand, e.g.: how should the client be returned in this scenario (e.g., by sending them a custom error message), or what happens when their token is expired/revoked
});
